text: q-0009's 'fewer LLM calls' premise holds on its EMISSION half but NOT (by the size proxy) on its SOLVER half. e-0012..e-0016 established that naming a frontier is ~88x cheaper than emitting a full proof and that 633 deep decls supply a >=2-wide named frontier; but none asked whether those subgoals are actually EASIER than the parent. e-0017 measures exactly that over the 633 wide-frontier targets: comparing each parent's CONCLUSION (recovered by a right-to-left top-level-':' scan, robust to the left-truncated binder prefix) against its top-level frontier subgoals. Decomposition does NOT reliably reduce per-subgoal difficulty: the largest frontier subgoal is a median ~90% of the parent conclusion's size (ratio_max p50 0.90, p90 3.0, mean 1.41), only 54.8% (347/633) of decls have EVERY subgoal smaller than the parent, and 45.2% (286/633) have at least one subgoal at least as large as the parent (e.g. MonotoneOn.union_right 1.5x, repr_opow_aux2 7.6x). The parent conclusion is often a short final claim (p50 43 chars) while the named haves spell out more verbose intermediate constructions. So a section-predictor+solver planner's potential advantage cannot be assumed to come from smaller/easier subproblems; it must come from INDEPENDENT solvability / search-space decomposition — which the eventual Modal pass@k comparison must measure directly. CAVEAT: chars/tokens are a size proxy for difficulty, not search space (a data-level bound like e-0010/e-0016, not pass@k).
